text: The surgeon examines the ileocolic area, observing endometrial implants in the ileum and noting that the appendix is replaced by an endometrial implant. The surgeon proceeds through the mesentery towards the appendix, assessing the margin on the cecum.

The surgeon examines the ileocolic area, observing endometrial implants in the ileum and the appendix, which is replaced by an endometrial implant. The surgeon navigates through the mesentery to the appendix, assessing the margin on the cecum for a potential appendectomy.

The surgeon clears the mesentery from the base of the cecum to ensure adequate space for the stapler. The stapler is then used to divide the tissue, taking a wedge of the cecum while avoiding the ileocecal valve lumen. The specimen is prepared for sending.

The surgeon clears the mesentery from the base of the cecum to ensure adequate space for the stapler. The stapler is then positioned carefully to avoid encroaching on the lumen of the ileocecal valve, with the intraoperative frozen section confirming endometriosis.

The surgeon divides a wedge of the cecum, over-sewing the staple line to prevent obstruction. The specimen is sent off for analysis. Attention shifts to an intramural implant on the ileum, where the surgeon plans to excise the lesion and close the defect with sutures, carefully following underneath the intramural lesion.

The surgeon targets an additional intramural implant on the ileum, carefully excising it with a wedge resection. The defect is then closed using interrupted 3-0 Vicryl sutures, ensuring the lesion is removed without full-thickness penetration.

The surgeon closes the incision transversely, ensuring proper apposition of the cut edges to prevent luminal narrowing. Sutures are placed at both corners before proceeding to the center to avoid the corners ducking in during closure. Additional sutures are applied to secure the closure.
